title: Methods of solving the system of equations for the energy gap in the revisited BCS theory of superconductivity
author: Anghel, Preda
description: The Bardeen-Cooper-Schrieffer (BCS) theory of superconductivity has been revisited in a series of papers [1], [2], [3] and in this context the equation for the energy gap was generalized to a system of integral equations. The physical consequences of this change are major, leading not only to the change of the critical temperature and of energy gap, but even to a change of the order of the phase transition and to multiple solutions for the energy gap. Nevertheless, finding the solutions of the proposed system of equations is much more complicated than solving the typical BCS gap equation and requires a careful analysis. This analysis is done here and consists of the following steps:‚Ä¢writing the system of equations at finite temperature (kBT comparable with the energy gap Œî) and in the low temperature limit (kBT‚â™Œî);‚Ä¢separate analysis of the equations and of their solutions in the two temperature ranges, (first) kBT‚â™Œî and (second) kBT comparable with Œî;‚Ä¢presenting the methods to consistenlty searching the solutions.

steps_raw.title: Method details
steps_raw.instruction: Let us detail here the methods used in Ref. [3]. The starting point is the BCS Hamiltonian [4](1)H^=‚àëks‚ààk(0)n^ks+‚àëklVklck‚Üë‚ÄÝc‚àík‚Üì‚ÄÝc‚àíl‚Üìcl‚Üë,written in terms of the creation ck,s‚ÄÝ and annihilation ck,s operators on the states determined by the quantum numbers (k,s) (concretely, k is the wavevector and s‚â°‚Üë,‚Üì is the spin projection). The free particle energy is ‚ààk(0) and the number operator is denoted by n^ks‚â°ck,s‚ÄÝck,s. We assume Vkl‚â°‚àíV if ‚ààk(0),‚ààl(0)‚ààIV‚â°[Œº‚àí‚Ñèœâc,Œº+‚Ñèœâc] and Vkl‚â°0 otherwise. Then, ŒºN^ is subtracted from the Hamiltonian (N^‚â°‚àëk,scks‚ÄÝcks) and H^M=H^‚àíŒºN^ was liniarized and diagonalized by the Bogoliubov-Valatin transformations [5,6],(2)H^M=‚àëk(Œæk‚àí‚ààk+Œîbk*)+‚àëk‚ààk(Œ≥k0‚ÄÝŒ≥k0+Œ≥k1‚ÄÝŒ≥k1)where Œæk‚â°‚ààk(0)‚àíŒº, ‚ààk‚â°Œæk2+Œî2, Œî is the energy gap, Œ≥k0=ukck‚Üë‚àívkc‚àík‚Üì‚ÄÝ, and Œ≥k1=vkck‚Üë‚ÄÝ+ukc‚àík‚Üì (Œ≥‚ÄÝ and Œ≥ are quasiparticle creation and annihilation operators, respectively). The coefficients uk and vk satisfy(3)|vk|2=1‚àí|uk|2=12(1‚àíŒæk‚ààk).

steps_raw.title: The method for finding the quasiparticle population at zero temperature
steps_raw.instruction: S1 is further divided into smaller sub-steps. In Step 1.1 (S1.1) we take a‚â§2. Therefore, we have only to look for the second solution. steps_raw.instruction: Physical quantities at zero temperature and special cases Once we determine the solution b of Eq.¬Ý(15b), we can proceed to Step 2 and calculate Œî directly from Eq.¬Ý(18). Noticing that the relative error in the determination of Œî/Œî0 is of the order 10‚àí4 or smaller, we can ignore the effect of finite A and A0 and calculate Œî for M=1 (i.e., A,A0‚Üí‚àû). We shall analyze the situation when a‚àà[0,2], since for a>2 there are no solutions, whereas for a 0. As mentioned above, for any a‚àà[0,2] there are two solutions, the first of which corresponds to b=0 and Œî=Œî0 (the typical BCS solution at zero temperature), whereas the second solution corresponds to b<0 and Œî<Œî0, as presented in Fig.¬Ý3. We observe that lima‚Üí0b(a)=‚àí‚àû, such that lima‚Üí0ab(a)‚â°lab is finite (and negative).
